Open in ServiceNow ↗
ServiceNow Data Model Reference — CSDM 5

Common Service Data Model v5

CSDM is ServiceNow's prescriptive framework defining which tables to use, how to relate records across the platform, and where the boundary between business and technical concerns sits. Version 5 restructures the model around a five-phase service lifecycle — adding a new Ideation & Strategy domain and introducing a cleaner separation between portfolio management and service delivery.

CSDM 4 → 5: What changed

The four classic domains (Design, Build, Manage Technical, Manage Business) are superseded by seven purpose-built domains organised along the service lifecycle. Ideation & Strategy is entirely new — powered by SPM tables (sn_align_core_product_idea, sn_align_core_planning_item). Manage Portfolio is now a distinct top-level domain. Two key table renames: Application Service is now Service Instance (cmdb_ci_service_auto); Technical Service is now Technology Management Service (cmdb_ci_service_technical). Foundation is unchanged.

10 Key Principles

  • Use simplified concepts — fewer, well-defined entities over sprawling custom tables
  • Design for reporting and analytics — data shaped for dashboards from the start
  • Prescribe the data relationships — explicit FK patterns, not ad-hoc links
  • Share the data model across products — unified access for all ServiceNow AI Platform products
  • Use clear definitions — each concept has a single, published definition
  • Share base-system tables — use OOB tables shipped by ServiceNow, not custom ones
  • Consistent data integrations — standard import patterns for all products
  • Speed adoption — phased Foundation→Crawl→Walk→Run→Fly maturity model
  • Enable data governance and process — ownership and quality by design
  • Provide practical user documentation — each product documents its CSDM usage

Foundation Domain

Foundation is the mandatory base layer — the core referential data that every other domain depends on. It must be populated accurately before any CSDM adoption work begins. A clean Foundation means consistent data everywhere else.

Highlighted table names are in your loaded schema — click to navigate.

Organisation

TablePurpose
core_companyCompanies and organisations
core_partnerPartner / supplier sub-type
cmn_departmentDepartments within a company
cmn_cost_centerCost centres for financial allocation
business_unitBusiness units (spans departments)

Location

TablePurpose
cmn_locationLogical locations (city/campus/office)
cmn_buildingPhysical building records
cmn_floorFloor within a building
cmn_roomRoom within a floor

People & Teams

TablePurpose
sys_userAll platform users
sys_user_groupTeams, assignment groups, approval groups
sys_user_grmemberUser ↔ group membership
sys_user_roleRoles granted to users

Products & Contracts

TablePurpose
ast_contractContracts with vendors / customers
cmdb_modelProduct model (hardware & software)
cmdb_model_categoryModel categories (server, laptop…)
cmdb_groupCMDB CI groups for dynamic membership

Foundation Life Cycle Processes

CSDM 5 defines five entity life cycle patterns. Each maps to a set of Foundation records.

Life Cycle typeStagesExample entities
ProductIdeation → Design → Operational → End of LifeSoftware, hardware product models
Tangible / PhysicalOrdered → In stock → In use → RetiredHardware assets, servers
Intangible / LogicalDraft → Active → RetiredBusiness apps, services, information objects
DocumentDraft → Review → Published → RetiredKnowledge articles, policies
LocationPlanned → Active → DecommissionedBuildings, floors, rooms

End-to-End Service Life Cycle

CSDM 5 organises the entire service landscape as a five-phase life cycle. Services are born in Ideation, shaped in Design, built in Build, operated through Service Delivery, and consumed via Service Consumption. The portfolio view spans all phases.

Highlighted table names are in your loaded schema — click to navigate.
💡
Phase 1

Ideation & Strategy NEW

Captures product ideas and planning items before any design work begins. Powered by Strategic Portfolio Management (SPM) tables — not operational CIs. Provides the "why" for everything downstream.

sn_align_core_product_idea sn_align_core_planning_item
📐
Phase 2

Design & Planning

Describes what the business needs at a capability and information level. Business applications are the primary output — they are the architectural "what" before any CI exists.

cmdb_ci_business_app cmdb_ci_business_capability cmdb_ci_information_object
🔧
Phase 3

Build & Integration

Tracks the SDLC: Agile development components, software packages, releases, DevOps pipelines, and APIs. The primary SDLC entity is cmdb_ci_sdlc_component. Note: cmdb_ci_appl is a discoverable infrastructure CI, not an application portfolio record.

cmdb_ci_sdlc_component cmdb_ci_appl cmdb_ci_spkg rm_release cmdb_ci_api sn_devops_change
⚙️
Phase 4

Service Delivery

Where services are instantiated and operated. Two CSDM service types: Technology Management Service (cmdb_ci_service_technical, technology-facing) and Service Instance (cmdb_ci_service_auto, runtime instances — discovered, query-based, or tag-based). Business Services are published to consumers from the Service Consumption domain.

cmdb_ci_service_technical cmdb_ci_service_auto cmdb_ci_query_based_service cmdb_ci_service_tags
🛒
Phase 5

Service Consumption

How users discover and request services. Business Services (cmdb_ci_service_business) are the top-level consumer-facing entries; Service Offerings describe what is available under each; the request catalog delivers fulfillment. Connects directly to ITSM & HRSD workflows.

cmdb_ci_service_business service_offering sc_cat_item sc_request sc_req_item sc_task
📊
Cross-cutting

Manage Portfolio

Spans all five phases. Tracks the service portfolio at the highest level — which services exist, their business value, and their lifecycle stage — without duplicating the operational records.

svc_portfolio pm_portfolio

Digital System Model — 4 Layers

Underpins how CSDM maps relationships between business processes and physical infrastructure.

LayerDescriptionExample tables
Process People & automation — workflows, tasks, approvals task wf_workflow
Service (Logical) Service instances and the dependency map cmdb_ci_service_auto cmdb_rel_ci
Functional Technical & functional capabilities, applications cmdb_ci_appl cmdb_ci_business_capability
Infrastructure Physical and virtual infrastructure CIs cmdb_ci_server cmdb_ci_computer cmdb_ci_network_gear
Highlighted table names are in your loaded schema — click to navigate.
NEW

Ideation & Strategy

The newest CSDM 5 domain. Captures product ideas and planning items before any design or build work begins. Powered by Strategic Portfolio Management (SPM) tables — these are not CMDB CIs. Owned by enterprise strategy and portfolio management teams.

TableEntityNotes
sn_align_core_product_ideaProduct IdeaSeed-stage concept, linked to a product or capability (SPM table)
sn_align_core_planning_itemPlanning ItemEpic / feature / initiative tracking (SPM table)

Design & Planning

Describes business architecture — capabilities the organisation needs and information it manages — independent of how those capabilities are implemented. Typically owned by Enterprise / Service Architecture teams.

TableEntityNotes
cmdb_ci_business_appBusiness ApplicationWhat the business uses — maps business need to technical realisation
cmdb_ci_business_capabilityBusiness CapabilityAbility the organisation must possess, technology-agnostic
cmdb_ci_information_objectInformation ObjectFormal description of managed data entities

Build & Integration

Tracks the SDLC: Agile development components, software packages, releases, APIs, and DevOps pipeline changes. Owned by application teams and DevOps. Connects directly to ITSM Change Management. Note: cmdb_ci_appl is an infrastructure CI (discoverable application instance), not an application portfolio record — do not use it to store managed application details.

TableEntityNotes
cmdb_ci_sdlc_componentAgile Development ComponentPrimary SDLC tracking entity in CSDM 5 Build & Integration
cmdb_ci_applApplication (CI)Discoverable installed software CI (infrastructure, not portfolio)
cmdb_ci_spkgSoftware PackageDistributable software unit
cmdb_ci_apiAPIManaged API endpoint, links to service layer
rm_releaseReleaseSDLC release container (ITSM Release Management)
sn_devops_changeDevOps ChangeDevOps pipeline change record linked to ITSM change

Service Delivery

Where services are instantiated, operated, and related to the infrastructure that runs them. Defines two CSDM service types: Technology Management Service (technology-facing) and Service Instance (runtime instances). Business Services are a Service Consumption concept. Owned by Service Management / SRE.

TableService TypeNotes
cmdb_ci_service_technicalTechnology Management ServiceTechnology-facing service, manages technology stacks and components
cmdb_ci_service_autoService InstanceRuntime instantiation of a service (parent of sub-types below)
cmdb_ci_service_discovered↳ Mapped / Manual ServiceManually mapped or Discovery-populated service instance
cmdb_ci_query_based_service↳ Dynamic CI GroupQuery-based service instance — membership defined by a CI query
cmdb_ci_service_tags↳ Tag-based ServiceService instance membership defined by CI tags

Service Consumption

How users discover, request, and receive services. Business Services are the top-level consumer-facing entries; Service Offerings define the contractual "what"; catalog items provide the ordering mechanism; requests and tasks track fulfillment. Owned by ITSM / ESM teams.

TableEntityNotes
cmdb_ci_service_businessBusiness ServiceBusiness-facing service published to consumers; extends cmdb_ci_service
service_offeringService OfferingFormal offering with SLAs, prices, and linked catalog items
sc_cat_itemCatalog ItemOrderable item in the service portal
sc_requestService RequestTop-level request container
sc_req_itemRequested Item (RITM)Individual fulfilment item within a request

Manage Portfolio

Cross-cutting domain that spans the entire lifecycle. Tracks which services exist in the portfolio, their business value, and their maturity stage. Owned by portfolio management / CIO office.

svc_portfolio pm_portfolio cmdb_ci_service_business

CSDM 5 Adoption Maturity Model

CSDM adoption follows a five-stage maturity path. Each stage builds on the previous — attempting to skip stages results in data quality issues and reporting gaps. The Foundation stage is mandatory and has no prerequisites.

Highlighted table names are in your loaded schema — click to navigate.
Foundation

Foundation — Core referential data

Establish the data that everything else references. Without clean Foundation data, every subsequent stage produces inaccurate reports and broken relationships. No prerequisites.

core_company business_unit cmn_department cmn_location sys_user_group sys_user cmdb_group cmdb_model ast_contract
Crawl

Crawl — Application focus

Map business applications to the technical CIs that implement them. Establish the Service Instance layer — the dependency map between apps and infrastructure. Prerequisite: Foundation complete.

cmdb_ci_business_app cmdb_ci_appl cmdb_ci_app_server cmdb_ci_service_auto
Walk

Walk — Technology Management Services

Introduce Technology Management Services (TMS) — the bridge between technical operations and service consumers. Add Service Offerings and Dynamic CI Groups to enable accurate impact analysis. Prerequisite: Crawl complete.

cmdb_ci_service_technical service_offering cmdb_ci_query_based_service cmdb_ci_server
Run

Run — Business Services & Portfolio

Publish Business Services to business stakeholders. Establish the Service Portfolio. This stage delivers full business-to-technology traceability — incident impact flows from infrastructure all the way to Business Service. Prerequisite: Walk complete.

cmdb_ci_service_business svc_portfolio pm_portfolio service_offering
Fly

Fly — Full CSDM

Activate Business Capabilities, Information Objects, a complete Request Catalog, and the Ideation & Strategy domain. This is full CSDM maturity — strategic planning data connects to operational reality end-to-end. Prerequisite: Run complete.

cmdb_ci_business_capability cmdb_ci_information_object sc_cat_item sn_align_core_product_idea sn_align_core_planning_item

Migration Guidance

When migrating from CSDM 4 or a custom model: (1) back up current data, (2) map existing attributes to CSDM 5 equivalents, (3) identify FK dependencies before renaming tables, (4) refactor reference fields, (5) migrate and validate data quality. Never migrate multiple stages simultaneously.

GBS / Enterprise Service Management Architecture

The GBS (Global Business Services) blueprint positions ServiceNow as the workflow data fabric — the single platform connecting omni-channel engagement with business operations and systems of record. Rather than each department running isolated service desks, GBS consolidates HR, Finance, Legal, Supply Chain, and IT delivery through a unified service model built on CSDM.

Highlighted table names are in your loaded schema — click to navigate.

Architecture Layers

LayerRoleServiceNow capability
Omni-channel Engagement Employees & customers reach services through any channel — portal, mobile, chat, email, voice Now Mobile, Virtual Agent, Employee Center
Workflow Data Fabric Single platform orchestrating cross-domain workflows; all data in one schema, no integration tax Now Platform core, Flow Designer, Integration Hub
GBS Service Lines HR, Finance, Legal, Supply Chain each deliver services through shared service centre patterns HRSD, FSM, Legal Service Mgmt, CSM, ITSM
Autonomous IT Detect-to-Correct + Request-to-Fulfil automation; AI-assisted remediation ITOM, AIOps, Predictive Intelligence
Systems of Record SAP, Workday, Salesforce, Oracle — integration via 200+ OOTB spokes Integration Hub, Spoke registry

GBS Service Lines — Key Tables

🏢 IT Service Management

The foundational service line. Manages incidents, changes, problems, and requests across the enterprise.

incident change_request problem sc_request task change_task

👥 HR Service Delivery

Employee lifecycle and HR case management — onboarding, offboarding, benefits, and employee enquiries.

sn_hr_core_case sn_hr_le_case sn_hr_core_activity sys_user

🔒 Security Operations

Ingests threat intelligence, manages security incidents and vulnerabilities end-to-end with automated triage.

sn_si_incident sn_vul_entry sn_si_task

📦 Asset & Configuration

Hardware and software assets tracked through their full lifecycle, backed by CMDB for topology context.

alm_asset alm_hardware alm_license cmdb_ci cmdb_ci_server

⚖️ Risk & GRC

Policy, risk, compliance, and audit — links controls directly to the CI and service data in CMDB.

sn_risk_risk sn_compliance_policy sn_audit_finding

🔭 IT Operations Management

Discovery populates CMDB; Event Management routes alerts to incidents; AIOps correlates and predicts.

em_alert discovery_status cmdb_ci_linux_server cmdb_ci_win_server

Integration Hub — 200+ OOTB spokes

The GBS blueprint relies on Integration Hub to synchronise ServiceNow with systems of record: Workday (HR master data), SAP (finance/procurement), Salesforce (CRM), Oracle (ERP), and cloud providers. Spokes write into Foundation tables — sys_user, core_company, cmn_location — keeping the CSDM data fabric consistent.

⚙ Settings
❓ Schema Explorer — User Guide

What is Schema Explorer?

Schema Explorer is an independent, community-built tool for visualising the table structure, relationships, and data model of any ServiceNow instance. It is not developed, endorsed, or affiliated with ServiceNow, Inc. in any way.

Load a schema JSON exported from your instance and immediately explore how tables connect through reference fields, inheritance, M2M relationships, and more — without needing to navigate the platform or read documentation manually.

Select any table to instantly see what it references, what references it, its full field list, and how it sits in the inheritance hierarchy.

Schema Map

A physics-based force-directed graph where tables are nodes and relationships are edges. Adjust Max Nodes, Hop Depth, and edge-type toggles to control what's visible. Best for exploring a table's neighbourhood in depth.

Additional view modes

Path Finder — finds shortest dot-walk paths between tables or to a specific field. Enable in Settings → Features.

Schema Diff — compares two schema exports to highlight added, removed, and changed tables. Enable in Settings → Features.

What you can do

✦ Explore which tables reference a given table and vice versa

✦ Trace inheritance chains through the extends hierarchy

✦ Discover M2M junction tables and named relationships

✦ Inspect fields, types, and scopes for any table

✦ Search by table name or field name across the entire dataset

✦ Filter the canvas with a multi-condition builder (scope, type, name, edge type, field count, custom prefix)

✦ Find the shortest dot-walk path between any two tables or to any field

✦ Compare two schema exports to see what changed between builds or upgrades

✦ Save named views and restore them instantly

✦ Export as PNG, SVG, JSON, Markdown, JSON-LD, OWL/Turtle, or OpenAPI YAML

Header at a glance

search boxFree-text search. The Tbl / Fld toggle inside the input switches between table-name and field-name search.
Schema MapSegmented view-mode control. Schema Map is always present. Path Finder and Schema Diff appear here when enabled in Settings.
◀ / ▶Navigation history. Step back and forward through your last 10 states — table selections, view-mode switches, filter changes, and density slider commits. Disabled until you have recorded at least two states. Keyboard shortcuts: Alt+← / Alt+→.
RefreshRe-render the graph preserving the current selection, filters, and node positions.
FitZoom and pan to fit all visible nodes into the viewport.
FilterOpens the dynamic condition builder. Add conditions to narrow the canvas and sidebar list by scope, table type, name, field name, edge type, field count, or custom prefix. Active conditions show a badge count. Hidden while Path Finder is active.
Export ▾Download schema data in multiple formats (JSON, Markdown, JSON-LD, OWL/Turtle, OpenAPI YAML) at either full-schema or current-neighbourhood scope, plus PNG and SVG canvas exports with background colour and opacity control. See Tips & Tricks for details on each format.
ReferenceOpen the ServiceNow Data Model Reference. Covers CSDM 5 architecture, domain breakdown, service lifecycle, adoption maturity path, and the ESM blueprint across six tabs. Table name chips throughout the reference are interactive — when a table exists in your loaded schema it lights up green; click it to close the modal and navigate directly to that table in the schema map.
GuideOpens this user guide.
SettingsToggle features and preferences. Settings persist across sessions via localStorage.

Footer

stats barShows total tables, total edges, currently visible tables, and the selected table name.
instance pillAppears after data is loaded. Shows the instance name and build. Click to open the Instance Info modal — full metadata, coverage statistics, and runtime info. When a diff schema is loaded, shows base → compare and the modal shows a side-by-side Stats Comparison.

Getting data into the explorer

The loading screen appears automatically when you open the tool. There are two ways to get started.

📄 Load File

Load a schema_graph.json file exported from your ServiceNow instance. Drag and drop one or more JSON files onto the drop zone, or click to browse.

Single file: drop one JSON — the standard output from the Background Script or Node.js extractor.

Multi-part: large instances split the export into a manifest file plus multiple .part*.json chunks. Select or drop all of them together; the loader reassembles them automatically.

See the Setup Instructions panel (in the Load File tab) for step-by-step guidance on running the exporter scripts on your instance.

🔬 Demo Dataset

Click Load Demo Dataset to load a pre-built sample covering ITSM, CMDB, HR, CSM, Risk, and Asset tables. Great for exploring the tool without a ServiceNow instance.

Schema Diff — loading a second schema

When Schema Diff is enabled (Settings → Features → Schema Diff) and you switch to the Diff view, a dedicated drop zone appears in the left sidebar. Drop a second JSON export there to compare it against the currently loaded schema.

The original (first) schema is the base. The second file you drop is the compare schema. The diff highlights what changed between them.

Use the clear button (✕) in the diff sidebar to remove the compare schema and return to the base view.

Navigating the visualization

Pan & Zoom

Scroll wheelZoom in and out
Click + dragPan the canvas
+ / − buttonsZoom in / out with the on-screen controls (right edge of canvas)
Fit (header)Zoom and pan to fit all visible nodes into view
MinimapClick or drag the minimap (bottom-right) to pan instantly. The green rectangle shows your current viewport.

Selecting tables

Click a nodeSelect a table. Expands the neighbourhood based on Hop Depth and active Edge Types. Inspector opens on the right. The simulation arranges neighbours in approximate concentric rings — hop-1 tables closest, hop-2 the next ring out, and so on.
Sidebar listClick any table name in the left panel to select and focus it. Click the selected table again to deselect.
Search boxType to filter the sidebar list and dim non-matching nodes. Use Tbl / Fld to switch between table-name and field-name search.
Click canvasClick empty canvas space to deselect and return to the full unfiltered view.
Right-click nodeContext menu: Focus neighbourhood · Deselect · Copy table name · Open in ServiceNow.

CMDB CI topology edges

When a table is selected, CI topology (cmdb_rel) edges always render with arrows pointing outward from the focused node, regardless of whether the focused table is the parent or child in each relationship. This gives a consistent "radiate outward" view — every arrow leaving the selected node means "this table relates to that one" in some direction.

The Inspector's CI topology section shows the true direction with perspective-aware labels ("Contains" vs "Contained by"). Use it to understand which way each relationship actually flows.

Searching by field

Click Fld in the search-box toggle to switch to field-name search. The sidebar narrows to tables that have a matching field; the canvas dims everything else.

Useful for questions like "which tables have an assignment_group field?" or "which tables reference sys_user via a field starting with opened_?"

Viewport indicators

When connected tables are off-screen, small pill-shaped indicators appear on the canvas borders pointing toward them. Click any pill to pan and zoom directly to that table.

Indicators only appear for tables connected to the currently selected table. They update automatically as you navigate.

Active Tables panel

When a table is selected, the Active Tables panel appears in the top-right of the canvas. It lists the selected table and all visible neighbours.

Click any neighbour row to hide or show that table. Hidden tables are crossed out but remain in the list. The selected table itself cannot be hidden. Hidden tables reset when you select a different table.

Navigation history

The ◀ and ▶ buttons in the header toolbar step backwards and forwards through your last 10 states — exactly like browser history.

A history entry is recorded when you: select or deselect a table, switch view modes, apply a scope filter or edge-type toggle, or commit a Hop Depth / Max Nodes slider change.

◀ BackReturn to the previous state. Disabled at the earliest recorded entry. Keyboard: Alt+←
▶ ForwardRe-apply the next state after going back. Disabled at the most recent entry. Keyboard: Alt+→

The history stack resets when you load a new schema, so it always reflects the current dataset only.

Compact view

The ◑ button in the top-right of the canvas toggles Compact view. When active, node labels and scope text are hidden and nodes shrink to their minimum size — useful for getting an overview of a large neighbourhood without individual labels cluttering the graph.

Click again (button reads ◑ Full view when compact is on) to restore labels.

Resizing panels

Both the left sidebar and the right Inspector panel can be resized by dragging the thin handle on their inner edge. Your preferred widths are saved automatically.

Double-click the resize handle to instantly reset that panel to its default width.

Saved Views

The Saved Views section in the left sidebar (when enabled in Settings → Features) lets you snapshot the current state — selection, scope filters, edge types, hop depth, max nodes, view mode — and restore it with one click.

Saved Views are stored per dataset (keyed by a fingerprint of the loaded schema), so views from one instance don't appear when you load another.

Filters & Edge Types

Density Controls

Max nodesLimits how many tables are shown when a table is selected. The most-connected neighbours are prioritised. Defaults to 10 on large datasets; increase for broader context. The slider ceiling tracks what's reachable given current filters. Your value is preserved when you deselect.
Hop depthControls how many relationship hops to follow from the selected table. Depth 1 = direct connections only. Depth 2 = connections-of-connections. Higher values reveal broader context but can pull in many tables quickly.

Application Scopes

Each table belongs to an application scope (e.g. global, sn_itsm, sn_cmdb). The Application Scopes section shows a read-only list of all scopes with their colour dots and table counts. Use the Filter + button to quickly add a scope condition to the Filters panel.

Filters

The Filters ▸ panel is a dynamic condition builder. Add as many conditions as you need — they combine with AND logic so only tables matching all conditions are shown on the canvas and in the sidebar list. A badge on the header shows how many conditions are active. All conditions persist in saved views.

Click ▸ to expand or collapse the panel. Click + Add condition to open the condition picker — already-added condition types are hidden from the menu so you can't add duplicates. Click × on a row to remove it. Clear all removes every condition at once.

Application ScopeFilter by one or more application scopes. Click scope pills to toggle them — active (highlighted) scopes are included. An empty selection means no scope restriction (all scopes pass).
Table TypeRestrict by table category: Regular (all sys_db_object tables, including CMDB CI tables), DB Views (database views from sys_db_view), or Custom (tables whose name starts with a configured custom prefix — same as the Is Custom condition).
Table NameMatch by technical table name or label. Click the operator button to cycle through Starts with, Contains, and Is (exact match). All three operators search both the technical name and the human-readable label.
Has FieldShow only tables that have at least one field whose technical name or label contains the search term. Useful for finding all tables that carry a specific field (e.g. approval_state, opened_by).
Has EdgeShow only tables that have at least one edge of the selected type. All 8 edge types are available: Reference (out), Reference (in), Extends (parent), Extends (children), M2M, Relationship, DB View, CMDB CI Topology.
Field CountFilter by the number of fields on the table. Set a min, a max, or both. Leave a field blank to skip that bound.
Is CustomShow only tables whose names start with a configured custom prefix (see Settings → Display → Custom prefixes). Requires the prefix list to be configured; by default this matches u_ and x_ tables. Equivalent to selecting Custom in the Table Type condition.

Cascading conditions

When two or more conditions are active, each subsequent condition's UI narrows to reflect only the values present after the previous conditions are applied:

  • Application Scope — scope pills show only scopes that appear in nodes passing all prior conditions, with live table counts.
  • Table Type — each pill shows the count of matching tables after prior conditions.
  • Has Field — a count hint next to the input updates as you type, showing how many of the remaining tables have that field.
  • Has Edge — the edge-type dropdown only offers edge types that exist on at least one node in the current filtered set.
  • Field Count — placeholder hints show the actual min/max field counts in the current set.

Filters also update the Max Nodes slider ceiling and the table count in the density info line.

The filter panel is hidden while Path Finder is active. The header search bar stays visible in all views — in Path Finder mode it dims matching canvas nodes but does not affect the from/to autocomplete (use the dedicated Path Finder inputs for that).

Edge Types legend

The Edge Types panel sits in the bottom-left of the canvas. Click any row to toggle that relationship type on or off. Inactive types are dimmed and struck through.

Reference to — outgoing reference fields. The selected table has a field that points to another table.
Referenced by — incoming references. Other tables have a field pointing to the selected table.
Inheritance — extends/parent relationship. One table inherits all fields from another (e.g. incident extends task).
M2M junction — many-to-many relationship via a junction table.
Named relationship — explicit relationship definitions (e.g. cmdb_rel_type).
DB view member — the table participates in a database view that joins multiple tables.
CI topology — runtime CI relationship from cmdb_rel_type_suggest. Structural, not dot-walkable.

Start with Reference to for the most common use case: tracing what data a table depends on. Add Referenced by to see impact — which tables break if this one changes.

Only active edge types are traversed during neighbourhood expansion. Toggling types off prunes the graph immediately without changing your selection.

Sort the table list

The Sort bar above the table list lets you sort by name (A→Z or Z→A) or by edge count (most/least connected). Applies immediately to the list and to active search results.

The Inspector panel

The Inspector opens on the right side whenever a table is selected. It shows detailed metadata about the selected table and all its relationships.

Properties

scopeThe application scope this table belongs to
coreWhether the table is a core platform table (not from a scoped app)
extendsThe parent table this table inherits from (if any). Click to navigate to the parent.
childrenNumber of tables that directly extend this table

Inheritance-aware fields

The Inspector handles both export modes automatically:

Flattened mode — each table's field list already includes inherited fields. Inherited fields are tagged with a small badge showing the ancestor that introduced them. Click the badge to navigate to that ancestor.

Delta mode — each table lists only its own fields. A separate Inherited fields section appears, grouping ancestor fields by source ancestor, sorted closest-first.

Collapsible sections

All sections except Properties start collapsed. Click a header to expand or collapse. Open/closed state is remembered per table as you navigate.

Fields (N)All own fields, sorted A→Z by display label. Shows technical name, display label, and field type colour-coded by data type.
References outTables this table has reference fields pointing to, with the field name
Referenced byTables that have reference fields pointing to this table
Child tablesTables that directly extend this table (immediate children in the inheritance hierarchy)
M2M relationshipsMany-to-many relationships via junction tables
Named relationshipsExplicitly defined relationship entries
DB viewsDatabase views this table participates in
CI topologySuggested CMDB CI relationships sourced from cmdb_rel_type_suggest. Labels are perspective-aware: when the inspected table is the parent in a relationship the label shows the parent verb (e.g. "Contains"); when it is the child, the child verb (e.g. "Contained by"). Click any entry to navigate to that table. Only present for tables that appear in the CMDB relationship catalog; absent on older instances without CMDB.

Clicking any table entry in the Inspector (References out, Referenced by, Child tables, etc.) immediately selects and navigates to that table in the graph.

Custom tables & fields (when "Highlight custom tables & fields" is enabled)

When the Highlight custom tables & fields setting is on (Settings → Display), tables and fields whose names match the configured prefixes (default: u_, x_) are visually distinguished:

CanvasCustom table nodes get a purple dashed border and purple label.
Inspector field listCustom fields show a purple custom badge next to the field name. The Fields section title also shows the count of custom fields (e.g. Fields (42, 7 custom)).
Sidebar table listCustom tables show a small custom badge next to their label.

Configure the prefix list in Settings → Display → Custom prefixes to match your organisation's naming conventions.

Copying field names

Click any field row in the Fields (or Inherited fields) section to copy its technical name to the clipboard. A small toast confirms the copy. Useful when writing encoded queries, business rules, or ACL conditions.

The row highlights on hover to indicate it is clickable. Clicking the ancestor badge or the Path Finder button on a row still navigates / opens Path Finder — the copy only fires when clicking the row itself.

Path Finder shortcut (when Path Finder is enabled)

Hover any field row in the Inspector and a small ⤳ button appears on the right. Click it to jump to Path Finder with the current table pre-filled as the source and that field as the target — the search runs automatically.

Diff mode inspector (when Schema Diff is active)

When a compare schema is loaded, selecting a changed table shows a side-by-side field comparison. Added fields are highlighted in green, removed fields in red, changed fields in amber. Fields that are identical in both schemas are shown without highlighting.

Settings

The Settings button opens a modal where you can toggle features and adjust behaviour. All settings persist in your browser's localStorage across page reloads.

Settings are organised into four categories: Features, Display, Behaviour, and Experimental.

A Reset to defaults button at the bottom of the modal reverts every setting to its factory state (a confirmation prompt appears first).

Features

Major features that add significant UI surface area. Most are off by default.

Saved ViewsOn by default. Adds the Saved Views section to the left sidebar. Save and restore named snapshots of your current selection, filters, hop depth, scope, and view mode.
Path FinderOff by default. Adds the Path Finder view mode — shortest dot-walk path between two tables or from a table to a specific field. See the Path Finder tab for full details.
Schema DiffOff by default. Adds the Schema Diff view mode — compare two schema exports to highlight added, removed, and changed tables. See the Schema Diff tab for full details.

Display

Control how information is presented on the canvas.

Font sizeDefault 100%. Scales text throughout the inspector, sidebar, and modals. Use − / + to step in 10% increments (range 80–150%). Persists across reloads and resets to 100% with "Reset to defaults".
Highlight custom tables & fieldsOff by default. Marks tables and fields whose names start with a configured prefix with a purple dashed border on the canvas, a custom badge in the inspector field list, and a badge in the sidebar table list. The field count in the inspector header also shows how many custom fields are present.
Custom prefixesComma-separated list of name prefixes used to classify custom tables and fields. Default: u_, x_ — covers user-created tables (u_) and scoped-app tables (x_). Change to match your organisation's naming conventions (e.g. u_, acme_). Applied when "Highlight custom tables & fields" is enabled. Persists across reloads.
Show field names on edgesOff by default. Renders the reference field name alongside each edge in Schema Map for the selected table's neighbourhood. Useful for tracing dot-walks visually; can clutter dense graphs.
Dim unrelated tables on hoverOff by default. When hovering a table in Schema Map, dims everything that isn't a direct neighbour. Transient — lifts when you move the cursor away.

Behaviour

How the app responds to your actions.

Auto-fit canvas on selectionOff by default. Smoothly recentres and zooms the canvas to fit the new neighbourhood whenever you select a table.
Clear search on selectionOn by default. Empties the search box when you click a table, so the next search starts fresh. Sidebar-list clicks bypass this — your filter is preserved.
Show inherited references in inspectorOff by default. When a table is selected, also shows the outgoing and incoming references of every ancestor in its inheritance chain, clearly grouped and labelled by ancestor. For example, selecting incident also shows task's references. Inherited sections start collapsed and have a subtle left-border accent.
Show inherited edges on canvasOff by default. Extends the above to pull ancestor edges into the canvas neighbourhood. Tables that reference task will appear when incident is selected. Can expand the graph significantly on deep chains — keep Hop Depth at 1. Has no effect unless "Show inherited references in inspector" is also on.
Show inherited refs in edge tooltipOff by default. When hovering a reference edge on the canvas, also shows reference fields inherited from ancestor tables that point to the same target. Each contributing ancestor appears as a separate group: ↳ inherited from Task: with up to 5 fields listed; additional fields are summarised as … +N more. The full inheritance chain is walked automatically — no depth limit.

Export

Settings that control export quality and limits.

Max PNG scaleDefault 50×. Sets the upper limit of the PNG resolution slider in the Export menu. Use − / + to step in 10× increments (range 10–200×). The browser's 16 MP pixel cap still applies regardless — an amber warning appears in the export menu when the chosen scale would be capped.

Experimental

These features work but may have rough edges — performance issues on large graphs, layout glitches, or unfinished UX. Try them and turn them off if anything misbehaves.

Expanded field viewOff by default. Shows the selected table's fields inline inside its node rectangle in Schema Map. Useful for quick schema inspection; expands node sizes significantly on tables with many fields — consider keeping Max Nodes low.
Advanced Path Finder configOff by default. Adds a Configuration panel to the Path Finder sidebar for tuning minimum/maximum path length and the number of alternatives returned. Requires Path Finder to be enabled.

Tips & Tricks

Common workflows

Impact analysisSelect a table → enable Referenced by → Hop Depth 2. Shows all tables that depend on this one, and their dependents.
Data lineageSelect a table → enable Reference to → Hop Depth 2–3. Traces the full chain of data dependencies.
Find child tablesSelect a table → enable Inheritance only. Reveals the full extension hierarchy below the selected table.
CMDB CI hierarchySelect cmdb_ci → enable Inheritance → Hop Depth 3–4. Reveals the full CI class tree.
"Which tables have this field?"Switch the search box to Fld mode and type the field name. The sidebar narrows to matching tables; the canvas dims the rest.
Snapshot a useful viewUse Saved Views in the sidebar to capture the current state. Restores selection, filters, hop depth, scope, and view mode.
Dot-walk pathEnable Path Finder (Settings → Features) and switch to the Path Finder view. Enter source and target, click Find. Copy the result dot-walk directly into your script or ACL.
Upgrade impactExport schema before and after an upgrade. Load the pre-upgrade JSON, switch to Schema Diff (Settings → Features), drop the post-upgrade JSON. See exactly which tables and fields changed.

Exporting data & views

The Export ▾ menu in the header offers several options:

Schema as JSONThe full loaded dataset. Share with a colleague — they load it via the Load File tab to see exactly what you see.
Neighbourhood as JSONJust the selected table and its currently-visible neighbours (respects scope, edge-type toggles, hop depth, and hidden tables). Great for a focused ticket or doc attachment.
Schema as MarkdownEvery table in the loaded schema rendered as a Markdown document — one ## heading per table with a field table (field name, type, label), plus sections for references, inheritance ("extended by"), M2M relationships, named relationships, CI topology, and DB view membership. Downloads as a .md file. Paste into Confluence, Notion, or any Markdown-aware wiki.
Neighbourhood as MarkdownSame Markdown format, but limited to the selected table and its currently-visible neighbours. Only available when a table is selected.
Schema as JSON-LDThe full schema as a JSON-LD document — tables become owl:Class resources and fields become owl:DatatypeProperty or owl:ObjectProperty resources, all linked via standard OWL/RDFS predicates. Useful for loading into a triplestore, knowledge-graph tool, or LLM context window. Downloads as a .jsonld file.
Neighbourhood as JSON-LDSame JSON-LD format, limited to the selected table and its visible neighbours.
Schema as OWL/TurtleThe full schema as an OWL ontology in Turtle serialisation — standard prefixes (owl:, rdfs:, xsd:, snp:). Tables become owl:Class resources; fields become owl:DatatypeProperty / owl:ObjectProperty with rdfs:domain and rdfs:range. All six edge types are represented: inheritance as rdfs:subClassOf; M2M as standalone sn:M2MRelationship resources; named relationships as sn:NamedRelationship resources; CI topology as sn:CiRelationship resources; and DB view membership as sn:viewIncludes / sn:memberOfView predicates on the class. Open in Protégé, ROBOT, or any OWL reasoner. Downloads as a .ttl file.
Neighbourhood as OWL/TurtleSame OWL/Turtle format, limited to the selected table and its visible neighbours.
Schema as OpenAPIThe full schema as an OpenAPI 3.0 YAML document. Each table gets Table API CRUD paths (GET /api/now/table/{name}, POST, GET /…/{sys_id}, PATCH, DELETE) plus a component schema with typed properties. DB views are read-only (no POST/PATCH/DELETE). A servers: block is added automatically when the instance URL is known. Standard query parameters are included per ServiceNow Table API docs: sysparm_query, sysparm_limit (default 10 000), sysparm_offset, sysparm_fields, sysparm_display_value, sysparm_exclude_reference_link, sysparm_suppress_pagination_header, sysparm_view; POST and PATCH also include sysparm_input_display_value. All six edge types appear as extensions: M2M as x-sn-m2m, named relationships as x-sn-relationships, CI topology as x-sn-ci-topology, DB view membership as x-sn-view-includes / x-sn-member-of-view. Reference fields use $ref (if the target is in scope) or x-sn-reference. Downloads as a .yaml file. For Postman import, the Neighbourhood scope is recommended — the full-schema export works but is very large.
Neighbourhood as OpenAPISame OpenAPI format, limited to the selected table and its visible neighbours. Recommended for Postman — imports directly as a ready-to-use collection with requests for each visible table. Reference $ref links resolve within the neighbourhood scope.

Background Script format note: The Background Script supports JSON, Markdown, and JSON-LD output formats. The OWL/Turtle and OpenAPI serialisers are too complex for the ES5/Rhino engine. For those formats, export your schema as JSON via the Background Script, then convert it in the viewer (Export → ↓ OWL/Turtle or ↓ OpenAPI) or via the Node.js extractor (--format=owl or --format=openapi).

Current view as PNG/SVGExports only what is visible in the current viewport. PNG resolution is set with a slider (1× to the configured max; default 50×, raise up to 200× in Settings → Export). An amber warning appears when the chosen scale would be capped by the browser's canvas size limit (16 MP on iOS Safari; significantly higher on desktop browsers). SVG is a scalable vector with inlined styles, editable in Illustrator, Inkscape, or Figma.
Full canvas as PNG/SVGExports the entire diagram regardless of your current scroll or zoom position — every visible node is included. Useful when you have panned away from part of the graph. The same PNG resolution slider applies as for the current-view export.
Background colourBelow the scale slider: a colour swatch, a hex input (#rrggbb), and an opacity slider (0–100%). Applies to all PNG and SVG exports. Drag the opacity to 0% for a fully transparent background (useful for overlaying on dark slides or documents) or any value between for a semi-transparent fill.

Neighbourhood JSON is only available when a table is selected — there is no neighbourhood without a selection. Schema and view exports work any time data is loaded.

Performance tips

For large datasets (5 000+ tables), keep Max Nodes below 50 and Hop Depth at 1 or 2. The force simulation is heavier with more nodes.

Use the Scope filter to narrow the dataset before selecting tables. This keeps neighbourhoods focused and reduces rendering time significantly.

Turn off Expanded field view when working with large flattened-schema tables — tables like cmdb_ci_service can have hundreds of fields and expand node sizes dramatically.

Use ⏹ Freeze layout (appears during simulation) to stop the physics engine early once the layout looks good enough.

Keyboard & interaction shortcuts

Alt+← / Alt+→Navigate back / forward through the last 10 states (same as the ◀ ▶ header buttons)
↑ / ↓ (Diff view)Move the keyboard cursor through the diff sidebar table list
Enter (Diff view)Select or deselect the currently focused diff list row
EscClose the Reference guide, User guide, or Settings modal; in Diff view also clears the current selection and keyboard cursor
Click canvasDeselect and return to full view (preserves Max Nodes)
Click selected in listDeselect the table (same as clicking empty canvas)
Right-click nodeContext menu: Focus · Deselect · Copy name · Open in ServiceNow
Drag nodeManually reposition a node — restarts physics briefly
Minimap dragDrag inside the minimap to pan the main viewport

Understanding the data

The schema is extracted from eight ServiceNow tables:

sys_db_objectAll tables — name, label, scope, and the super_class reference that drives the inheritance graph. The tables stat counts only these sys_db_object entries; DB views (from sys_db_view) are counted separately in the DB views row. No active filter — every row is included. (The admin UI at sys_db_object_list.do applies a display filter that hides some internal system tables; the exporter bypasses it and fetches all rows.)
sys_dictionaryEvery active field — element name, column label, data type, reference target, and key attributes (mandatory, primary, virtual). Qualification: element must be non-empty (table-level metadata rows have a blank element and are always excluded) and the field must not be explicitly inactive. Encoded query (Node.js / Table API): elementISNOTEMPTY^active!=false — matches both active=1 (explicitly active) and active=NULL (never written, the default for the vast majority of field definitions). The Background Script uses addQuery('active', true), which behaves identically via GlideRecord's truthy-null matching.
sys_glide_objectExtended field-type definitions — scalar type, display label, class name, and custom attributes. Powers the colour-coded type badges shown in the Inspector and expanded field view. No filter — all rows are included.
sys_m2mMany-to-many junction table definitions (from/to tables, junction table, field names). No filter — all rows are included.
sys_db_view + sys_db_view_tableDatabase view definitions and the member tables that participate in each view. No filter on either table — all rows are included.
sys_relationshipNamed relationship definitions between tables. No filter — all rows are included.
cmdb_rel_type_suggestPermitted CI-class topology relationships (e.g. "Computer Hosts Application"). Rendered as the CI topology edge type. Absent on older instances without CMDB. No query filter — all rows are fetched — but two post-fetch rules apply: (1) rows whose relationship type label cannot be resolved are dropped; (2) each logical relationship produces two raw rows (one with parent=true, one with parent=false) — SchemaBuilder deduplicates these mirror pairs into a single canonical entry, so the final CI topology count is roughly half the raw row count in ServiceNow.

Five additional data sources are queried for the instance metadata block shown in the footer pill and Instance Info modal:

sys_propertiesBuild metadata — platform version name, build tag, and build date. The Background Script reads glide.buildname, glide.buildtag, and glide.builddate via gs.getProperty(). The Node.js extractor queries sys_properties via the Table API but must use different keys — com.glide.embedded_help.version, glide.buildtag.last, and glide.builddate.last — because the original BG-script properties are not accessible through the Table API.
sys_cluster_stateNode count (number of active application nodes in the cluster). No filter — COUNT(*) of all rows.
v_pluginCount of active plugins installed on the instance. Filter: active=active — the active column on this table is a string, not a boolean; active plugins carry the literal value 'active'.
sys_packageCount of active application packages. Filter: active=true. Background Script only — sys_package is excluded from the Table API (and the Stats API), so the Node.js extractor cannot query it and will show null for this stat.
sys_languageCount of active languages. Filter: active=true.

The edge count in the sidebar counts all relationship types combined.

The data model reflects your instance at extraction time. Re-export after deploying new applications or upgrades to get a current snapshot.

Path Finder

Path Finder is an optional view mode that computes the shortest dot-walk path between two tables, or from a table to a specific field on a related table. It uses Dijkstra's algorithm with inheritance-aware weights and returns up to five alternative paths ranked by total cost.

Path Finder is disabled by default. Enable it in Settings → Features → Path Finder to make the third segmented-control button and the inspector ⤳ links appear.

How to use it

Once enabled, click Path Finder in the view-mode segmented control at the top.

The left sidebar replaces the table list with the Path Finder inputs:

Mode→ Table finds a path from a source table to a target table. → Field finds a path from a source table to a specific field on any reachable table.
FromSource table. Type to autocomplete from the loaded dataset.
ToEither a target table (in Table mode) or a field name (in Field mode). Dot-walks like incident.company.sys_id work — the autocomplete resolves each segment.
FindRuns the search. The DAG view appears on the canvas, and the dot-walk result with a copy button shows in the sidebar.

How paths are ranked

Inheritance is free. Hopping from a child table up to a parent (via extends) costs nothing because parent fields are directly accessible on the child. Reference hops each cost one step.

Paths through M2M, named-relationship, and DB-view edges are excluded — they're not legitimate dot-walks at the script/ACL level.

The primary path is the cheapest result; alternatives are ranked by total cost, then by segment count. Use alternatives when the primary path goes through a field that's typically empty in your data and you need a fallback.

Under the hood. The primary path is computed with Dijkstra's algorithm on the directed weighted graph (edges: extends outbound with cost 0, reference outbound with cost 1; M2M, rel, and view edges removed). Alternatives are then enumerated with Yen's K-shortest paths algorithm (K = 5), which repeatedly runs Dijkstra on graphs with selected edges/nodes blocked, then merges and ranks the candidate paths by total cost, breaking ties by segment count.

Reading the DAG view

The canvas shows a left-to-right directed acyclic graph. The source is on the left (tagged FROM), the target on the right (tagged TO).

The primary path is drawn in solid wasabi-green. Alternative paths are dimmed; expanding any alternative in the sidebar highlights it in cyan on the canvas.

Click any node in the DAG to open the Inspector with that table's details, without leaving Path Finder.

Copying the result

Every result shows two copy-ready strings, each with its own ⧉ button:

Dot-walkThe plain reference chain — e.g. assigned_to.department. Paste into scripts, ACL conditions, email templates, or UI policies.
Encoded queryThe same path formatted as a ServiceNow encoded query fragment — e.g. assigned_to.department=<value>. Replace <value> with the desired filter value and paste directly into a GlideRecord query, list filter, or condition builder.

Hop exclusions

Some tables act as hubs — sys_user, cmdb_ci, sys_choice — with hundreds of reference edges. The pathfinding algorithm routes through them as cheap intermediates, which can produce noisy results where nearly every path passes through the same hub table.

Two exclusion granularities are supported:

Table exclusion — type a bare table name (e.g. sys_user) and select from the autocomplete. The entire table is skipped as an intermediate waypoint but can still be used as the start or end of a path.

Field exclusion — type in dot-walk notation (e.g. task.assigned_to) to exclude a single reference field while leaving the target table reachable via other fields. The autocomplete switches to field mode once you type a dot, listing reference fields on the table before the dot (including inherited fields). Field exclusion chips appear in cyan to distinguish them from table chips.

Active exclusions appear as chips above the add input. Click × on any chip to re-include it, or use Clear all to remove all exclusions at once. The search result updates automatically whenever an exclusion changes.

Exclusions persist across reloads (stored in localStorage).

Schema Diff

Schema Diff compares two schema JSON exports side by side, highlighting tables that were added, removed, or changed between them. Use it to understand the impact of an upgrade, a new application install, or any other schema change.

Schema Diff is disabled by default. Enable it in Settings → Features → Schema Diff. A new Diff button appears in the view-mode control at the top.

How to use it

Load your first (base) schema via the Load File tab as usual, then:

1. Enable Schema Diff in Settings → Features → Schema Diff.

2. Click Diff in the view-mode control to switch to the Diff view.

3. Drop a second JSON export onto the drop zone in the left sidebar. This becomes the compare schema.

4. The graph updates immediately: nodes are colour-coded by their diff status, and the sidebar lists all changes.

The two schemas don't need to be from the same instance, but they must use the same JSON format (same exporter version). Comparing a pre-upgrade and post-upgrade export of the same instance is the most common use case.

Reading the graph

Green outlineTable exists in the compare schema but not in the base — it was added.
Red outlineTable exists in the base schema but not in the compare — it was removed. Shown dimmed on the canvas.
Amber outlineTable exists in both schemas but has differences in its fields or relationships — it was changed.

Unchanged tables have no coloured outline. Use Show all in the sidebar to display them alongside changed tables, or toggle it off to focus only on changes.

The Diff sidebar

Summary rowShows the count of added, removed, and changed tables at a glance.
Header searchThe toolbar search bar (Tbl mode) filters the diff list in real time — type a table name or label to narrow results. The Fld mode is not applied to the diff list but still dims matching nodes on the canvas.
Filter tabsAll · Added · Removed · Changed — click to narrow the list to one category. Works together with the header search and the inline filter box.
Inline filter boxType in the filter box below the summary to narrow the list by table name or label in real time. The ✕ button clears the filter. Applies on top of the active category tab and the header search.
Show allToggle to include or exclude unchanged tables in the graph.
Table listAll changed tables listed by category. Click any row to select that table and open it in the Inspector. Relationship-change rows inside a table entry are also clickable — clicking one navigates to the related table. Use ↑ / ↓ to move a keyboard cursor through the list, Enter to select or deselect the focused row, and Esc to clear the selection.
Clear (✕)Remove the compare schema and return to the normal base-only view.
Export method warningIf the compare schema was exported with a different method than the base (e.g. background-script vs table-api), an amber banner appears below the drop zone. When one side is a Table API export, the banner also notes that tables or fields from privately-scoped apps may be absent due to cross-scope ACL restrictions — these gaps are expected and are not a data error.

Inspector in diff mode

Click any changed table to open the Inspector. For changed tables, the Inspector shows a side-by-side field comparison:

Added fieldsFields present in compare but not in base
Removed fieldsFields present in base but not in compare
Changed fieldsFields present in both but with different types, labels, or references

For added tables, the Inspector shows the full field list of the new table. For removed tables, it shows the base field list.

Field rows show the human-readable label beneath the technical name when the two differ (e.g. Opened by under opened_by), and the type column shows the readable type label (e.g. Reference instead of reference).

If a table's relationships changed, a Relationships section appears below the fields, listing each added or removed edge with its type, the related table's technical name, and its human-readable label. Clicking a relationship row navigates to that table.

Stats comparison

Once a compare schema is loaded, the footer pill updates to show base → compare. Click it to open the Schema Comparison modal, which shows:

• Identity and export metadata for both schemas

• A stats comparison table: tables, fields, references, M2M, extends edges, scopes, and more — with base value, compare value, and Δ (delta) for each stat. Increases are green, decreases are red, unchanged values are muted.

The tables count reflects sys_db_object entries only — DB views are listed separately in the DB views row. A background-script export may show more tables than a Table API export if ACL-restricted scoped-app tables are visible only to the admin session.

Instance
